questions.question: Which of the following inherited syndromes is associated with an increased risk of colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Lynch syndrome, also known as hereditary nonpolyposis colorectal cancer, is the most common cause of hereditary colorectal cancer, due to mutations in DNA mismatch repair genes.

questions.question: Which lifestyle factor is most clearly associated with increased risk of colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: High consumption of red and processed meats has been consistently associated with an increased risk of colorectal cancer, probably due to the formation of N-nitroso compounds and heterocyclic amines during cooking.

questions.question: Which of the following genes is commonly mutated in familial adenomatous polyposis?
questions.explanation: Familial adenomatous polyposis is caused by germline mutations in the APC gene, which acts as a tumor suppressor. Its loss leads to the formation of multiple adenomatous polyps in the colon.

questions.question: Which of the following personal histories confers the greatest risk of colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Inflammatory bowel disease, especially long-standing ulcerative colitis, significantly increases the risk of colorectal cancer due to chronic inflammation and epithelial dysplasia.

questions.question: What age is considered an independent risk factor for sporadic colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Age over 50 years is the main risk factor for sporadic colorectal cancer, so it is recommended to start screening from that age in the average-risk population.

questions.question: Which of the following inheritance patterns is present in Lynch syndrome?
questions.explanation: Lynch syndrome follows an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, meaning that one mutated copy of a mating repair gene (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2) is enough to increase the risk.

questions.question: What is the most common symptom in right colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Right colon cancer usually presents with iron deficiency anemia due to chronic occult bleeding, unlike left colon cancer that causes changes in intestinal habit or red blood.

questions.question: What symptom is characteristic of left colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Ribbon or ribbon stools are typical of left colon cancer due to the reduction in the caliber of the intestinal lumen due to the tumor, causing partial obstruction.

questions.question: Which of the following findings is associated with rectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Rectal bleeding or red blood in the stool is the most common symptom of rectal cancer, due to the proximity of the tumor to the anal canal and its tendency to bleed.

questions.question: What general symptom usually accompanies advanced colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Unintentional weight loss is a common constitutional symptom in advanced colorectal cancer, secondary to the catabolic effect of the tumor and intestinal obstruction.

questions.question: What is the most common initial clinical manifestation of colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: The change in intestinal rhythm (alternating constipation or diarrhea) is the most common initial manifestation, especially in left colon tumors that alter motility.

questions.question: What symptom may indicate intestinal obstruction due to colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Abdominal distension with cramping pain and absence of bowel movements is a classic sign of intestinal obstruction, a frequent complication in advanced colorectal cancer.

questions.question: What is the most common anatomical location of colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: The rectum is the most common location of colorectal cancer, representing approximately 30% of cases, followed by the sigmoid colon.

questions.question: Which segment of the colon is second in frequency of colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: The sigmoid colon is the second most common location after the rectum, due to greater exposure to fecal carcinogens and prolonged stasis.

questions.question: What is the most common location of colorectal cancer in patients under 50 years of age?
questions.explanation: In young patients under 50 years of age, colorectal cancer tends to be located more in the right colon (ascendant and cecum), with a more aggressive presentation.

questions.question: What portion of the large intestine is most affected by hereditary colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: In hereditary syndromes such as hereditary nonpolyposis colorectal cancer (Lynch), the right colon is the most affected, frequently in the cecum and ascending colon.

questions.question: What is the most common segment of colorectal cancer in the general population over 70 years of age?
questions.explanation: In people over 70 years of age, the rectum continues to be the most common location, although the incidence in the right colon increases with advancing age.

questions.question: Which location of colorectal cancer is most frequently associated with BRAF mutation?
questions.explanation: The BRAF V600E mutation is more common in right colon cancers, especially in sporadic tumors with microsatellite instability and serrated phenotype.

questions.question: What is the rationale for colorectal cancer screening with fecal occult blood?
questions.explanation: Fecal occult blood screening seeks to detect blood that is not visible macroscopically, indicative of bleeding neoplastic or adenomatous lesions in the colon.

questions.question: What type of fecal occult blood test is currently recommended because of its highest sensitivity?
questions.explanation: The fecal immunohistochemical test (FIT) is more sensitive and specific than the guaiac test, detects human hemoglobin, and does not require dietary restrictions.

questions.question: How often is fecal occult blood screening recommended in the average-risk population?
questions.explanation: The annual fecal immunohistochemical test (FIT) is the recommended frequency for the average-risk population aged 50 years and older, according to current guidelines.

questions.question: What finding in the fecal occult blood test should lead to a colonoscopy?
questions.explanation: A positive fecal occult blood test result requires colonoscopy to identify and biopsy lesions such as adenomatous polyps or cancer.

questions.question: What is an important limitation of the guaiac fecal occult blood test?
questions.explanation: The guaiac test can give false positives for foods with peroxidase (red meat, fruits) and false negatives for vitamin C, limiting its accuracy.

questions.question: At what age range does fecal occult blood screening begin for an average risk population?
questions.explanation: Current guidelines recommend starting screening at age 45 for the average-risk population, although some maintain 50 years as the traditional age.

questions.question: What is the test of choice for the detection of colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Colonoscopy is the gold standard for the detection of colorectal cancer because it allows you to directly visualize the colonic mucosa and perform biopsies or polypectomies during the same procedure.

questions.question: Which colonoscopic finding is associated with a higher risk of malignancy?
questions.explanation: The sessile adenomatous polyp, especially if it is large or with high-grade dysplasia, presents a higher risk of malignant transformation, with colonoscopy being essential for its detection and resection.

questions.question: What is the main limitation of colonoscopy for population screening?
questions.explanation: Colonoscopy requires rigorous intestinal preparation and is an invasive procedure that carries risks such as perforation or bleeding, which limits its acceptance and massive application in population screening.

questions.question: What interval is recommended to repeat a colonoscopy with normal results?
questions.explanation: For a screening colonoscopy with a normal result and no additional risk factors, the guidelines recommend repeating the study in 10 years, since the progression of polyps to cancer is usually slow.

questions.question: Which technique improves the detection of flat lesions during colonoscopy?
questions.explanation: Indigo carmine chromoscopy highlights the mucosal surface, facilitating the identification of flat or sessile lesions that could go unnoticed with conventional white light during colonoscopy.

questions.question: What is the most feared complication of therapeutic colonoscopy?
questions.explanation: Intestinal perforation is the most serious complication of therapeutic colonoscopy, especially during polypectomy, requiring urgent surgical intervention and increasing morbidity and mortality if not detected in time.

questions.question: What anatomical structure defines the T3 stage in colorectal cancer according to TNM?
questions.explanation: In the TNM classification, T3 corresponds to tumor invasion through the muscularis propria to the pericolorectal tissues, without reaching the serosa or neighboring organs.

questions.question: What is the equivalent of Dukes C stage in the TNM classification?
questions.explanation: Dukes C stage is defined by the presence of metastases in regional lymph nodes, which in TNM is equivalent to any T with N1 or N2 and M0.

questions.question: In the Dukes classification, stage B implies:
questions.explanation: Dukes B stage is characterized by a tumor that passes through the intestinal wall (transmural invasion) but without lymph node involvement or distant metastasis.

questions.question: What TNM stage corresponds to a tumor that invades the visceral serosa?
questions.explanation: T4a in the TNM classification of colorectal cancer indicates tumor invasion of the visceral serosa (visceral peritoneum), which represents a locally advanced stage.

questions.question: What is the main prognostic difference between Dukes A and Dukes B?
questions.explanation: Dukes A is limited to the wall without completely penetrating it, while Dukes B involves transmural invasion. The depth of invasion is the factor that differentiates them, not the nodes or metastases.

questions.question: What does the N mean in the TNM classification of colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: In the TNM system, the N (node) evaluates the presence and extent of metastases in the regional lymph nodes, with N0 being without involvement and N1/N2 with increasing involvement.

questions.question: What is the main clinical utility of carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) in colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: CEA is mainly used to monitor tumor recurrence after curative surgery in colorectal cancer, since it is neither sensitive nor specific for diagnosis or screening.

questions.question: What benign condition can falsely elevate serum CEA levels?
questions.explanation: Active smoking is a known cause of false CEA elevation, which limits its diagnostic specificity and should be considered when interpreting results.

questions.question: In which type of cancer does CA 19-9 have the greatest diagnostic sensitivity?
questions.explanation: CA 19-9 is the tumor marker of choice for pancreatic cancer, although it can also be elevated in colorectal cancer and other gastrointestinal neoplasms.

questions.question: What is the main limitation of CA 19-9 as a tumor marker?
options: Elevation in patients with obstructive jaundice
questions.explanation: CA 19-9 is elevated in benign cholestasis, especially in obstructive jaundice, which reduces its specificity to differentiate benign from malignant disease.

questions.question: What tumor marker is recommended to measure together with CEA in the follow-up of metastatic colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: CA 19-9 is used together with CEA in the monitoring of metastatic colorectal cancer, since both can provide complementary information on tumor progression.

questions.question: What factor limits the usefulness of CEA as a screening test in the general population?
questions.explanation: CEA has low sensitivity and specificity for early colorectal cancer, with frequent false positives for benign conditions, which is why it is not recommended for population screening.

questions.question: What is the standard surgical treatment for stage I colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: In stage I colorectal cancer, endoscopic local resection or polypectomy is sufficient when the tumor is T1N0M0 and complete resection with negative margins is achieved.

questions.question: What surgical procedure is performed in stage III right colon cancer?
questions.explanation: Right hemicolectomy with regional lymphadenectomy is the standard surgical treatment for stage III right colon cancer, as it allows adequate oncological resection of the tumor and affected lymph nodes.

questions.question: In stage II rectal cancer, when is low anterior resection indicated?
options: Tumor in the lower third of the rectum
Tumor in the upper third of the rectum
Tumor in the middle third of the rectum
questions.explanation: Low anterior resection is performed for tumors of the middle third of the rectum, allowing the anal sphincter to be preserved when possible, combined with total excision of the mesorectum.

questions.question: What is the indicated surgery for stage IV colorectal cancer with resectable liver metastases?
options: Local resection of the primary tumor
questions.explanation: In selected patients with stage IV colorectal cancer and resectable liver metastases, synchronous or two-stage colectomy and liver resection offers the best curative option.

questions.question: Which surgical approach is preferable in stage III rectal cancer after neoadjuvant treatment?
questions.explanation: Total excision of the mesorectum is the surgical standard for stage III rectal cancer after neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy, reducing local recurrence.

questions.question: What criteria contraindicate curative surgery in stage IV colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Unresectable bilobar liver metastases contraindicate curative surgery, since it is not possible to resect all metastatic disease with oncological intent.

questions.question: What is the standard adjuvant chemotherapy regimen for stage III colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: The FOLFOX regimen (5-fluorouracil, leucovorin and oxaliplatin) is the standard of adjuvant chemotherapy in stage III colorectal cancer, based on trials that demonstrated benefit in disease-free survival.

questions.question: What biomarker is evaluated to decide the duration of adjuvant chemotherapy in stage III?
questions.explanation: Microsatellite instability (MSI) is a key biomarker; tumors with high MSI have a better prognosis and may require less adjuvant chemotherapy, avoiding unnecessary toxicity.

questions.question: What is the recommended duration of adjuvant chemotherapy with FOLFOX in stage III?
questions.explanation: The standard duration of adjuvant chemotherapy with FOLFOX in stage III colorectal cancer is 6 months, although 3 months may be considered in low-risk patients depending on the regimen and tolerance.

questions.question: What characteristic adverse effect limits the use of oxaliplatin in the adjuvant setting?
questions.explanation: Peripheral neuropathy is the dose-limiting adverse effect of oxaliplatin, manifesting as paresthesias and dysesthesias that can become chronic and affect the patient's quality of life.

questions.question: In which stage III subgroup is reducing adjuvant chemotherapy to 3 months considered beneficial?
questions.explanation: In patients with low-risk stage III (T1-3 N1), reduction to 3 months of adjuvant chemotherapy with FOLFOX or CAPOX is considered acceptable, with lower toxicity and similar efficacy in studies.

questions.question: What drug is used as an oral alternative to adjuvant 5-fluorouracil infusion?
questions.explanation: Capecitabine is an oral prodrug of 5-fluorouracil, approved in combination with oxaliplatin (CAPOX) as an alternative to FOLFOX in adjuvant chemotherapy for stage III colorectal cancer.

questions.question: What is the most important criterion to consider a liver metastasis from colorectal cancer resectable?
questions.explanation: The fundamental criterion for resectability is to achieve a complete resection (R0) with a negative margin, preserving sufficient functional liver parenchyma.

questions.question: What imaging technique is considered gold standard for evaluating colorectal liver metastases?
questions.explanation: Magnetic resonance imaging with hepatobiliary contrast is the most sensitive and specific technique for the characterization of liver metastases, surpassing CT and PET.

questions.question: In synchronous liver metastases from colorectal cancer, what is the most accepted surgical strategy?
questions.explanation: Combined synchronous resection of the primary tumor and liver metastases is feasible and safe in selected patients, avoiding sequential surgeries.

questions.question: What is the main objective of neoadjuvant chemotherapy in resectable liver metastases?
questions.explanation: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy seeks to reduce the size of metastases to facilitate an R0 resection with preservation of sufficient liver parenchyma.

questions.question: What factor is associated with a worse prognosis after resection of colorectal liver metastases?
questions.explanation: The presence of positive lymph nodes in the primary colorectal tumor is an independent adverse prognostic factor for survival after hepatectomy.

questions.question: What is the expected 5-year survival rate after successful resection of liver metastases?
questions.explanation: With R0 resection and appropriate patient selection, 5-year overall survival reaches approximately 40-50%, much higher than palliative management.

questions.question: Which of the following genes is most frequently mutated in Lynch syndrome?
questions.explanation: Lynch syndrome is due to germline mutations in DNA mismatch repair genes, with MLH1 and MSH2 being the most frequently affected.

questions.question: What type of inheritance does classic familial adenomatous polyposis present?
questions.explanation: Classic familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP) is inherited in an autosomal dominant manner, caused by mutations in the APC gene, with high penetrance.

questions.question: What is the risk of colorectal cancer in patients with Lynch syndrome who do not undergo surgery?
questions.explanation: Lynch syndrome confers a cumulative lifetime risk of colorectal cancer of 40-80%, with early onset, often before age 50.

questions.question: What is the management of choice in familial adenomatous polyposis with multiple adenomas?
questions.explanation: In familial adenomatous polyposis with multiple adenomas (>100), prophylactic colectomy is recommended to prevent the inevitable development of colorectal cancer.

questions.question: What clinical criteria are used to diagnose Lynch syndrome?
questions.explanation: The Amsterdam criteria (I and II) are the classic clinical criteria for the diagnosis of Lynch syndrome, based on a family history of colorectal cancer and other tumors.

questions.question: What gene is mutated in attenuated familial adenomatous polyposis?
questions.explanation: Attenuated familial adenomatous polyposis is also due to mutations in the APC gene, but with milder phenotypic expression and fewer polyps.

questions.question: What is the imaging standard for monitoring metastatic colorectal cancer?
options: CT scan of the chest, abdomen and pelvis
questions.explanation: Contrast-enhanced computed tomography of the chest, abdomen and pelvis is the study of choice to detect metastatic recurrence in the follow-up of colorectal cancer, according to international guidelines.

questions.question: When is the first surveillance colonoscopy after curative resection of colorectal cancer recommended?
questions.explanation: The first surveillance colonoscopy is performed one year after curative resection, to detect metachronous lesions or luminal recurrence; Subsequent intervals depend on findings.

questions.question: What tumor marker is routinely used in monitoring colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) is the standard serum marker to monitor recurrence in colorectal cancer, with moderate sensitivity but broad recommendation in guidelines.

questions.question: What is the response to a high CEA without visible lesions on standard images?
questions.explanation: In the event of elevated CEA with negative CT, PET-CT is the next recommended test to locate occult disease, since it combines metabolic and anatomical information.

questions.question: What factor is associated with a higher risk of early recurrence of colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Lymphovascular invasion is a high-risk histopathological factor that predicts early recurrence, regardless of stage, and warrants more intensive follow-up.

questions.question: What is the expected 5-year overall survival after curative resection of stage III colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: In stage III (positive nodes), the 5-year overall survival is 50-60% with surgical treatment and adjuvant chemotherapy, reflecting the risk of systemic recurrence.
